Participants will receive hands-on training with PredictionModels, an enterprise-grade computational platform that integrates structure-based drug design, ADMET prediction, and clinical trial outcome forecasting into a unified workflow. Through guided exercises, learners will build predictive pipelines, evaluate model performance against curated benchmark datasets, and interpret outputs in the context of real medicinal chemistry decisions. The platform's modular architecture allows trainees to experiment with custom feature engineering, transfer learning approaches, and uncertainty quantification methods that have become indispensable in modern computational pharmacology.

A central case study throughout the course follows the development trajectory of PulmoFix, a respiratory therapeutic whose preclinical optimization benefited substantially from AI-driven lead identification and toxicology screening. Examining this real-world program, students will trace how computational predictions translated into wet-lab validation milestones, what surprises emerged during in vivo studies, and how iterative feedback loops between data scientists and bench chemists accelerated candidate selection. This case study illustrates both the promise and the limitations of current AI methodologies, providing a balanced perspective grounded in operational reality.

The course is led by an expert faculty drawing on decades of combined industry experience. Lead instructor Oceanus brings deep expertise in cheminformatics and generative chemistry, having pioneered several frameworks now considered standard practice in pharmaceutical machine learning. Co-instructor Haemon contributes complementary specialization in clinical biostatistics and Bayesian trial design, ensuring participants understand how AI methods integrate with rigorous statistical reasoning required by regulatory authorities such as the FDA and EMA.

By course completion, participants will be able to critically evaluate AI vendor claims, design and supervise computational drug discovery projects, communicate effectively with cross-functional teams blending data science and traditional pharmacology expertise, and contribute meaningfully to strategic decisions about adopting emerging technologies. Assessments include practical coding assignments, case-based group projects, and a capstone presentation in which learners propose AI-augmented development plans for hypothetical therapeutic candidates.
a6bbfd79:
This comprehensive course on Quality Assurance Methods is designed for pharmaceutical professionals seeking to deepen their expertise in modern quality systems, regulatory compliance, and the practical application of validation methodologies in production environments. Participants will explore the full lifecycle of pharmaceutical quality assurance, from raw material sourcing and in-process controls to final product release testing and post-market surveillance. The curriculum balances theoretical foundations with hands-on case studies drawn from real manufacturing scenarios, ensuring that learners can immediately translate concepts into actionable workflows within their own organizations.

A central component of the course involves the integration of digital tools into traditional quality workflows. Learners will gain proficiency with QAAutomation, a platform engineered to streamline batch record review, deviation tracking, and CAPA management. Through guided exercises, participants will configure validation protocols, build electronic checklists, and generate audit-ready documentation. Special emphasis is placed on data integrity principles aligned with ALCOA+ requirements, ensuring that every digital record meets the rigorous expectations of global regulatory authorities including the FDA, EMA, and PMDA.

To anchor abstract concepts in tangible products, the course uses ImmuneX Pro as a recurring case study. Students will examine its quality control specifications, stability indicating methods, and release testing strategy, walking through how each parameter is justified under ICH Q8, Q9, and Q10 frameworks. This applied perspective allows participants to understand how risk-based thinking informs every quality decision, from analytical method validation to supplier qualification and change control management throughout the product lifecycle.

The course is co-led by two seasoned instructors who bring complementary perspectives. Jason contributes more than fifteen years of experience in sterile manufacturing and aseptic process validation, while Medea specializes in regulatory inspections and quality systems auditing, having supported numerous pre-approval inspections across multiple jurisdictions. Their combined insights provide learners with both an operational and a strategic lens on quality assurance, equipping them to navigate complex compliance challenges with confidence.

By the conclusion of the course, participants will be able to design and execute robust validation master plans, lead deviation investigations, conduct internal audits, and contribute meaningfully to continuous improvement initiatives within their quality units. The program is suitable for QA specialists, manufacturing supervisors, validation engineers, and regulatory affairs professionals seeking advanced credentials. Upon successful completion of assessments and a capstone project, participants receive a certificate recognizing their mastery of contemporary quality assurance methods in pharmaceutical operations.
767976aa:
The Cloud Infrastructure Security course is a comprehensive professional development program designed specifically for pharmaceutical industry practitioners who manage, architect, or audit cloud-based systems supporting drug development, manufacturing, and commercialization workflows. Participants will gain a deep understanding of how to apply security best practices to multi-tenant cloud environments while maintaining compliance with regulatory frameworks such as 21 CFR Part 11, GxP guidelines, and global data protection standards. The curriculum emphasizes practical application through case studies drawn from real pharmaceutical operations, ensuring that learners can immediately translate theoretical knowledge into operational improvements within their organizations.

A central theme of the course is identity and access management as the foundational layer of cloud security. Learners will work hands-on with IdentityHub, exploring how role-based access controls, federated authentication, and privileged access workflows can be configured to protect sensitive research data and manufacturing systems. Through guided labs, students will provision identities, design least-privilege policies, and implement audit trails that satisfy both internal compliance teams and external regulatory inspections. Particular attention is paid to lifecycle management of service accounts and the integration of single sign-on with laboratory information systems.

The course uses a running case study centered on the production and distribution infrastructure for PulmoFix, a respiratory therapeutic whose supply chain depends on tightly integrated cloud platforms. Through this case study, participants will examine threat models specific to pharmaceutical workloads, including ransomware targeting batch records, intellectual property exfiltration, and supply chain compromises affecting cold-chain telemetry. Each module concludes with a tabletop exercise where students must respond to a simulated incident, document their findings, and recommend remediation measures aligned with corporate risk appetite.

Course leadership is provided by two distinguished instructors with complementary expertise. Io brings extensive experience in cloud architecture and zero-trust network design, having led security transformations at several global biopharmaceutical organizations. Co-instructor Chiron contributes deep regulatory and validation knowledge, focusing on how cloud security controls map to computer system validation expectations and qualification documentation requirements.

By the conclusion of the program, participants will be equipped to evaluate cloud service providers, design defense-in-depth architectures appropriate for validated environments, lead incident response activities, and communicate effectively with quality assurance, IT operations, and executive leadership. Assessment includes a capstone project in which learners produce a security architecture review for a hypothetical pharmaceutical workload, complete with risk register, control mapping, and continuous monitoring strategy suitable for presentation to a steering committee.
5d65508b:
The Engineering All-Hands convened at our Cambridge, MA campus, drawing together software engineers, platform architects, data scientists, and DevOps specialists from across BioNova's global engineering organization. The full-day event served as both a strategic checkpoint and a cultural touchstone, providing teams an opportunity to align on technical roadmaps, celebrate shipped milestones, and surface cross-cutting challenges that span our increasingly interconnected technology stack. Attendance exceeded prior years, reflecting the growing scale of our engineering function and the expanding scope of computational work supporting our therapeutic pipeline.

The morning keynote was delivered by Hygieia, who framed the year ahead through the lens of One BioNova, our cross-functional initiative to unify research, clinical, and commercial data platforms into a coherent engineering fabric. The talk emphasized the importance of shared infrastructure standards, reusable component libraries, and a common identity layer that will enable engineers to move fluidly between domains without rebuilding foundational tooling. Several anecdotes drawn from recent integration sprints illustrated how the initiative has already reduced duplicated effort across discovery and clinical operations teams.

A highlight of the agenda was the technical deep-dive session on computational support for NeuraLink-7, our investigational neurology candidate currently advancing through late-stage development. Engineering leads walked through the bespoke pipelines built to handle high-throughput biomarker data, the real-time monitoring dashboards deployed to clinical sites, and the machine learning models being refined to support patient stratification. The session underscored how engineering excellence directly translates into faster, more reliable insights for the program's medical and regulatory stakeholders.

In the afternoon, Cerberus led a candid panel on platform reliability, drawing from recent incident retrospectives to discuss what world-class operational maturity looks like at BioNova's scale. The conversation touched on observability investments, on-call rotation health, and the cultural shifts required to move from reactive firefighting to proactive system stewardship. Engineers across all levels contributed questions, making the session one of the most engaged of the day.

Closing remarks from Teucer tied the day's themes together, reinforcing the connection between disciplined engineering practice and patient impact. The all-hands concluded with a networking reception, recognition of internal contributors, and a forward-looking commitment to quarterly engineering forums that will sustain the momentum generated in Cambridge throughout the coming year.
5916e93a:
BioNova is pleased to announce its upcoming Tech Talk: AI in Pharma, a half-day symposium dedicated to exploring how artificial intelligence and modern data infrastructure are reshaping pharmaceutical research, clinical operations, and translational medicine. The event will be hosted at our research campus in Cambridge, MA, and will bring together engineers, data scientists, clinical leads, and external collaborators for a series of talks, live demonstrations, and roundtable discussions. Whether you are an early-career researcher curious about machine learning workflows or a seasoned scientist evaluating AI integration into your therapeutic programs, the agenda has been designed to deliver practical, technically rigorous content.

The keynote will be delivered by Hephaestus, our principal architect for enterprise data platforms, who will provide a deep dive into DataLake v2. The talk will cover the platform's evolution from a centralized warehouse into a federated, governance-aware lakehouse capable of supporting petabyte-scale genomics, real-world evidence, and high-content imaging datasets. Attendees will learn how schema enforcement, lineage tracking, and on-demand feature engineering have accelerated downstream model development across multiple therapeutic areas.

Following the keynote, Tiresias will present a case study on how predictive analytics has been applied to the clinical development program for Oncora SC, our investigational subcutaneous oncology candidate. The session will examine how patient stratification models, adverse event signal detection, and adaptive trial design have collectively reduced enrollment timelines while strengthening the integrity of efficacy readouts. Expect candid discussion of the failure modes encountered during validation, including the limits of synthetic control arms and the operational challenges of deploying inference pipelines into regulated environments.

The afternoon track will be moderated by Urania, who leads our responsible AI working group. Her panel will tackle the harder questions: model explainability for regulatory submissions, bias auditing in patient-derived datasets, and the human-in-the-loop frameworks that keep clinical decision support tools both useful and accountable. Panelists will include representatives from biostatistics, regulatory affairs, and external academic partners.

The event concludes with a poster reception featuring contributions from interns and rotating fellows, followed by an informal networking hour. Registration is open to all BioNova employees and invited guests; remote attendance via livestream is also supported, with recordings made available afterward through the internal knowledge portal.
eac5c4ea:
Hackathon 2025 convened in Cambridge, MA over a three-day sprint that brought together computational biologists, regulatory specialists, software engineers, and bench scientists from across the organization. The event was structured around a series of cross-disciplinary challenges designed to accelerate internal tooling, surface novel therapeutic hypotheses, and harden infrastructure ahead of upcoming audits. Participants were grouped into thematic tracks spanning data engineering, clinical analytics, and platform security, with each track culminating in a live demonstration before a panel of senior reviewers. The Cambridge venue, chosen for its proximity to several academic collaborators, allowed for ad-hoc breakout sessions that extended well into the evening hours.

One of the most anticipated tracks centered on platform security and was anchored by SOC2 Compliance Remediation, a project that has been steadily reshaping how the company manages access controls, audit logging, and vendor risk. Teams competed to prototype automated evidence-collection pipelines, with several submissions targeting the manual bottlenecks that have historically slowed quarterly attestations. Halitherses served as the track captain, guiding teams through the nuances of control mapping and helping them translate raw telemetry into auditor-ready artifacts.

The therapeutic discovery track produced equally compelling results. Several teams worked with anonymized datasets associated with Genova RNA, exploring whether transcriptomic signatures could be leveraged to predict patient stratification more reliably. A parallel team focused on PulmoFix, building a lightweight simulation harness to model dose-response curves under varied pulmonary conditions. Hecate2 contributed deep expertise on the underlying assay data and helped multiple teams refine their statistical assumptions.

Mentorship and judging responsibilities were shared across senior staff, with Demeter overseeing the final review committee. The committee evaluated submissions on technical rigor, feasibility of productionization, and alignment with strategic roadmaps. Several winning prototypes have already been earmarked for incubation within their respective business units, including a continuous-monitoring agent that will likely accelerate compliance reporting timelines.

Beyond the formal competition, Hackathon 2025 fostered a strong sense of community. Lightning talks, poster sessions, and informal "office hours" gave junior contributors direct access to leadership, while evening receptions surfaced collaborations that may yield formal proposals in the coming quarter. Organizers have already begun planning the 2026 edition, with an emphasis on broader participation from international research sites.
707cc905:
The Architecture Review Board convened at our Cambridge, MA campus for a full-day working session focused on aligning enterprise technology decisions with strategic business objectives. This recurring forum brings together principal architects, engineering leadership, security stakeholders, and platform owners to evaluate proposed designs, ratify reference architectures, and adjudicate exceptions to established standards. The Cambridge location was selected specifically to facilitate in-person collaboration with the East Coast research and platform engineering teams, while remote participation was extended to colleagues across other geographies.

The session was chaired by Prometheus, who opened the agenda with a retrospective on architectural decisions ratified during the previous quarter and a forward-looking briefing on emerging technology bets. As board chair, Prometheus emphasized the need for tighter coupling between architectural governance and delivery cadence, particularly as the organization scales its data platform and AI-enabled research workflows. Co-facilitating the security and controls portion of the agenda was Peisistratus, who walked the board through pending threat-model reviews and proposed updates to the secrets management standard.

A substantial portion of the morning was dedicated to SOC2 Compliance Remediation, where the program lead presented a remediation roadmap addressing audit findings related to logging coverage, access review automation, and change management evidence. The board discussed trade-offs between centralized control plane investments and federated team-level controls, ultimately endorsing a hybrid model that preserves engineering velocity while satisfying auditor expectations. Several action items were assigned, including a follow-up architectural decision record on tenant isolation patterns and a deep-dive review of the proposed evidence collection pipeline.

The afternoon shifted to One BioNova, the company's cross-functional initiative to unify customer-facing experiences, internal developer platforms, and shared data products under a coherent technology strategy. The board reviewed the proposed capability map, debated boundaries between product domains, and provided guidance on the sequencing of platform consolidation work. Particular attention was paid to identity federation, API contract governance, and the migration path for legacy systems whose retirement is on the multi-year horizon.

The session closed with a roundtable on architectural debt, knowledge sharing practices, and onboarding for newly appointed board members. Minutes, decision records, and updated reference materials will be published to the architecture portal within five business days, and follow-up working sessions have been scheduled to advance the open action items before the next quarterly convening.
5b0b1999:
The most recent Sprint Demo Day, hosted at our Cambridge, MA campus, brought together engineering, research, and operations teams for a full afternoon of cross-functional showcases. Held in the newly renovated atrium auditorium, the event drew over two hundred attendees in person, with additional remote participants joining from our European and Asia-Pacific hubs. The agenda featured fifteen sprint teams presenting deliverables completed during the preceding two-week iteration, ranging from preclinical data pipeline improvements to revamped clinical trial dashboards.

The day opened with framing remarks from Hestia, who outlined how each demo connected back to the strategic objectives of One BioNova. Her introduction emphasized the importance of removing silos between research informatics, regulatory affairs, and commercial analytics — themes that recurred throughout the showcase. Attendees were encouraged to ask probing questions and to vote on which demonstrations best advanced the unified platform vision.

Among the most discussed presentations was a live walkthrough led by Amphion2, who demonstrated a new automated data harmonization service capable of reconciling laboratory information management system records with electronic data capture exports in near real time. The demo included a live failure-injection scenario, showing how the system gracefully degrades and notifies stewards when schema mismatches are detected. Several questions from the audience focused on how this service would integrate with existing master data management governance, and the team committed to publishing follow-up architectural decision records within the week.

The afternoon block was anchored by a deep-dive from Procris, whose squad unveiled a prototype clinician-facing portal for adverse event triage. The prototype incorporated feedback gathered during three rounds of contextual inquiry with safety physicians and pharmacovigilance scientists. Notably, the team showed how natural language summarization could reduce average case review time by an estimated thirty-five percent, a figure that prompted spirited discussion about validation requirements for any AI-assisted regulated workflow.

Closing remarks reinforced that Sprint Demo Day is not merely a checkpoint but a deliberate forum for celebrating incremental progress, surfacing dependencies, and aligning roadmaps across the portfolio. Recordings, slide decks, and the demo voting results have been posted to the internal collaboration hub, and follow-up working sessions for each prioritized integration have already been scheduled over the next sprint cycle.
658c8c79:
The annual Drug Discovery Symposium, hosted in Cambridge, Massachusetts, has become one of the most anticipated gatherings on the BioNova calendar, drawing computational chemists, translational scientists, regulatory specialists, and external academic collaborators into a multi-day program of plenary talks, poster sessions, and roundtable discussions. This year's event was organized by Athena, who serves as the program chair and curated a deliberately interdisciplinary agenda spanning early-stage target discovery through late-phase clinical readouts. The Kendall Square venue, with its proximity to MIT and the broader biotech corridor, made it possible to draw walk-in attendance from regional partners as well as international delegations.

The opening keynote was delivered by Philoctetes, who walked the audience through the architectural evolution of MolecularForge, BioNova's generative chemistry platform. His talk emphasized how the platform's reinforcement-learning backbone has compressed lead-identification timelines and produced novel scaffolds that are now advancing through preclinical evaluation. Several attendees noted that the live demonstration of retrosynthetic planning was a highlight of the morning session.

A focused track later in the day examined the clinical trajectory of ImmuneX Pro, BioNova's flagship immunomodulator. Clinical lead Atalanta presented updated Phase II efficacy and safety data, including subgroup analyses across immunocompromised cohorts. Her presentation closed with a candid discussion of post-marketing surveillance strategies and the operational lessons learned from the program's adaptive trial design.

An afternoon governance panel addressed something less glamorous but increasingly central to drug discovery operations: the intersection of data integrity, audit readiness, and platform validation. Panelists referenced the ongoing SOC2 Compliance Remediation initiative as a case study in how research-supporting infrastructure must evolve in parallel with scientific ambition. The discussion underscored that modern discovery pipelines depend on demonstrably trustworthy systems, and that compliance investment is no longer separable from R&D velocity.

The symposium closed with a networking reception along the Charles River, where attendees continued conversations sparked during the day's sessions. Organizers indicated that recordings, slide decks, and a curated proceedings volume will be made available to registered participants in the weeks following the event, with planning for next year's program already underway.
42f9032c:
The annual Compliance Training Day convened at our Cambridge, Massachusetts campus, bringing together regulatory affairs specialists, clinical operations leads, project managers, and senior scientists for a full-day immersion in evolving global compliance standards. The event opened with a welcome address from Apollo, who framed the day's agenda around three pillars: regulatory rigor, ethical clinical conduct, and transparent data stewardship. Attendees included cross-functional staff from research, manufacturing, and commercial divisions, all of whom completed mandatory modules on Good Clinical Practice (GCP), Good Manufacturing Practice (GMP), and the latest amendments to FDA 21 CFR Part 11 governing electronic records and signatures.

The morning sessions focused on case-based learning drawn directly from active pipeline programs. A highlight was a deep-dive workshop centered on Oncora, our flagship oncology initiative, where compliance leads walked participants through the documentation requirements specific to combination therapy trials. Poseidon, serving as the project's regulatory liaison, presented a detailed walkthrough of the investigational new drug (IND) submission package, emphasizing audit-readiness practices that have proven successful during recent inspections. Particular attention was paid to chain-of-custody requirements for Oncora Combo, the lead asset progressing through Phase II evaluation, given the operational complexities of dual-agent dispensing and reconciliation.

Following lunch, attendees rotated through breakout sessions covering pharmacovigilance reporting timelines, adverse event documentation, and EU Annex 11 considerations for multinational studies. Dionysus led a particularly engaging tabletop exercise simulating an unannounced regulatory inspection, challenging teams to retrieve source documents, sponsor correspondence, and deviation logs within compressed timeframes. The exercise reinforced the importance of maintaining contemporaneous records and well-organized trial master files.

The afternoon also featured a panel discussion on emerging compliance challenges, including the integration of artificial intelligence into clinical decision support, decentralized trial methodologies, and harmonization between FDA and EMA expectations. Panelists addressed audience questions on data integrity in remote monitoring environments and the appropriate use of real-world evidence in regulatory submissions.

The day concluded with a certification ceremony, during which all attendees received updated compliance credentials valid for the next twelve months. Feedback collected at the close indicated strong satisfaction with the practical, scenario-based format, and planning has already commenced for next year's event, which will expand to include sessions on post-marketing surveillance and lifecycle management strategies for approved therapeutics.
5f4682ed:
The Platform Migration Workshop, held in Cambridge, MA, brought together engineering leads, computational chemists, and infrastructure architects for an intensive three-day working session focused on transitioning Bionova's legacy modeling stack to a unified, cloud-native foundation. Attendees gathered at the company's flagship innovation center on Kendall Square, where the agenda balanced deep technical deep-dives with collaborative whiteboarding sessions designed to surface migration risks early and align cross-functional teams around a shared roadmap.

The workshop opened with a keynote from Prometheus, who framed the migration not as a lift-and-shift exercise but as a fundamental rearchitecting of how computational pipelines interact with data lakes, orchestration layers, and downstream analytics. The discussion emphasized backward compatibility for in-flight studies, cost governance under elastic compute models, and a phased cutover schedule designed to minimize disruption to ongoing discovery campaigns. Breakout groups then convened to map dependencies across more than two dozen internal services slated for retirement, replacement, or refactoring.

A significant portion of the second day was dedicated to MolecularForge, which represents the most architecturally complex workload targeted in the first migration wave. Participants reviewed the current monolithic deployment, identified bottlenecks in the simulation orchestration layer, and prototyped a containerized service mesh approach intended to scale ensemble runs across heterogeneous hardware. Live demonstrations illustrated how a redesigned job scheduler could reduce queue times for high-throughput screening campaigns while preserving the deterministic reproducibility that regulated research demands.

Technical sessions were co-facilitated by Echo, whose expertise in distributed data engineering shaped the conversation around metadata cataloging, lineage tracking, and the federated identity model that will govern access controls post-migration. Particular attention was paid to ensuring that experiment provenance — long a pain point under the legacy system — would be captured automatically through standardized event streams rather than ad-hoc logging conventions. Hands-on labs allowed engineering teams to experiment with the proposed schemas against representative datasets drawn from recent pipeline runs.

The workshop concluded with a synthesis session led by Daedalus, who consolidated outcomes into a prioritized action register, assigned workstream owners, and established the cadence for follow-up reviews. Key deliverables included a draft migration runbook, a risk matrix covering data integrity and compliance concerns, and a communication plan for stakeholders across research, IT, and quality assurance. Participants left Cambridge with concrete next steps and a renewed alignment on the strategic value of platform consolidation.

          "foundational": "You run structured interviews and review existing artefacts to articulate user problems and current workflows for a single feature area.",
          "working": "You independently lead discovery for substantial features, synthesising user, scientific, and business inputs into clear problem statements and success metrics.",
          "practitioner": "You guide discovery across complex, cross-functional initiatives, framing problems in ways that reshape roadmaps and align research, product, and engineering.",
          "expert": "You set how Bionova approaches problem discovery in regulated, scientific domains, coaching others and influencing portfolio-level investment decisions."
        }
      },
      "agent": {
        "name": "problem-discovery",
        "description": "Guidance for clarifying ambiguous problems with users and stakeholders before committing to solutions.",
        "useWhen": "scoping new initiatives or features where the problem is not yet well understood",
        "focus": "Producing a precise, evidence-backed problem statement and success criteria.",
        "readChecklist": [
          "Read the initial brief, request, or hypothesis triggering the work.",
          "Identify the primary user and stakeholder groups and their goals.",
          "Review existing data, analytics, or research relevant to the problem.",
          "Check prior solutions, tickets, or post-mortems addressing similar issues.",
          "Identify constraints from regulation, safety, or scientific validity.",
          "Plan discovery activities such as interviews, shadowing, or data analysis.",
          "Define how success and failure of any future solution would be measured."
        ],
        "confirmChecklist": [
          "Confirm the problem statement is written from the user's perspective with concrete context.",
          "Confirm assumptions are listed and distinguished from validated facts.",
          "Confirm key user journeys and pain points are documented with evidence.",
          "Confirm success metrics and guardrail metrics are explicit.",
          "Confirm out-of-scope areas and non-goals are stated.",
          "Confirm findings are reviewed with representative users and stakeholders.",
          "Confirm the artefact is stored where downstream teams can find and reference it."
        ]
      }
    },
    {
      "id": "rapid_prototyping",
      "name": "Rapid Prototyping",
      "human": {
        "description": "Builds quick, low-cost prototypes to test ideas, de-risk technical choices, and accelerate learning. Emphasises clarity about what a prototype is for and when it should be discarded or hardened.",
        "proficiencyDescriptions": {
          "awareness": "You build small throwaway scripts or UI sketches to explore an idea under direction, understanding that prototypes are not production code.",
          "foundational": "You construct focused prototypes to answer specific questions about feasibility or usability for a single feature, with guidance on scope.",
          "working": "You independently choose appropriate prototyping approaches, set clear learning goals, and translate findings into concrete design or engineering decisions.",
          "practitioner": "You lead prototyping for high-uncertainty initiatives, orchestrating experiments that de-risk strategy and influence cross-team direction.",
          "expert": "You shape how Bionova uses prototyping across the portfolio, embedding rapid experimentation into how scientific and product bets are evaluated."
        }
      },
      "agent": {
        "name": "rapid-prototyping",
        "description": "Guidance for building targeted prototypes that maximise learning per unit of effort.",
        "useWhen": "validating feasibility, usability, or scientific assumptions quickly and cheaply",
        "focus": "Answering specific questions with the smallest possible artefact while avoiding accidental productionisation.",
        "readChecklist": [
          "Read the hypothesis or question the prototype must answer.",
          "Identify the decision the prototype will inform and who will make it.",
          "Define what 'good enough' looks like for evidence and fidelity.",
          "Choose a stack or tool optimised for speed over robustness.",
          "Identify any real data needed and how to obtain it safely.",
          "Set a strict time-box and scope before starting implementation.",
          "Plan how results will be captured and shared."
        ],
        "confirmChecklist": [
          "Confirm the prototype directly addresses the stated hypothesis.",
          "Confirm shortcuts and known gaps are documented clearly.",
          "Confirm no sensitive or regulated data is exposed beyond approved boundaries.",
          "Confirm findings are summarised with evidence and recommendations.",
          "Confirm the prototype is labelled as non-production and not deployed by default.",
          "Confirm follow-up actions, including discard or hardening plans, are agreed.",
          "Confirm learnings are shared with relevant teams and stakeholders."
        ]
      }
    }
  ]
}
